column2: Changing plant functional diversity over the last 12,000 years provides perspectives for tracking future changes in vegetation communities
column7: NA
column8: In this study, they are able to link modern plant traits to fossil pollen assemblages in southeast (SE) Australia and to reconstruct long-term changes in vegetation community strategies in response to past climates within an Australian vegetation context.
column9: The goal in this study is to quantify  long-term changes in plant community ecological strategies and examine the role of associated direct climatic drivers of change (changes in temperature, El Niño/Southern Oscillation (ENSO) and Southern Westerly Winds (SWW)-driven precipitation) and indirect climatic drivers of change (changes in sea levels) in the vegetation of SE Australia through time.

column64:
Their results show that FD in SE Australia strongly relates 
to climate, especially moisture changes and that climate has been the primary driver of vegetation change or distribution (including vegetation openness) in SE Australia at least on a millennial scale. The vegetation of most regions show the highest FD within the wetter early to mid-Holocene and only the Bass Strait islands, and possibly the entire eastern part of SE Australia, showed some degree of functional resilience (increasing FD trend) during the drier late Holocene. Furthermore, their results generally support the diversity–stability hypothesis and that differential functional response of species to disturbance is more important than species diversity to maintain ecosystem stability and the degree of FD-driven stability is greater in continental areas than on small islands.

1)To construct a harmonized, high-latitude fossil pollen and macrofossil dataset across North America, Europe, and Northern Asia.

2) To adapt and refine a standardized biomization scheme designed to differentiate complex Arctic tundra and boreal forest categories.

3) To evaluate the geographic extent of changes in the northern treeline, the distribution of cold steppe, and structural variations in tundra types during key paleoclimatic intervals.
column28: North American Pollen Database, European Pollen Database
column64:
The adapted high-latitude biomization scheme successfully reconstructed modern potential vegetation across the circum-polar zone, accurately distinguishing between sub-types of tundra (e.g., shrub tundra vs. prostrate dwarf-shrub tundra) and boreal forests.

Mid-Holocene: Documented a significant northward shift of the forest-tundra boundary across Northern Eurasia. In Siberia, the boreal treeline migrated north by 100 to 200 km, driven by warmer summers. In contrast, the treeline in North America (Canada and Alaska) was close to its modern position or showed only minor local shifts, illustrating a strong regional asymmetry in Holocene thermal maximum responses.

Last Glacial Maximum: Characterized by extreme cold and widespread aridity. Boreal forest biomes were completely absent north of 55°N. Instead, the landscape was dominated by an extensive, open, and heterogeneous matrix of cold steppe, graminoid tundra, and prostrate dwarf-shrub tundra across Beringia and unglaciated Eurasia, reflecting a major restructuring of Arctic ecosystems.

column64:
Their results suggest that while pollen of herbaceous taxa
 in fens reflects vegetation at local fen scale, the pollen of woody taxa is likely to reflect larger landscape scale forest vegetation. Dividing woody and herbaceous taxa in pollen-based diversity estimates improves our understanding of spatial scales reflected in palaeodiversity reconstructions.
Correlations between pollen- and plant-based estimates of functional and phylogenetic diversity indicated that pollen data reflected the functional and phylogenetic aspects of plant communities reasonably well.
The largest changes in the FD and PD of herbaceous taxa were related to the bog-fen transition around 7 ka. Our results show that climate and other abiotic processes have considerably influenced community FD and PD through the 9.2 ka.

column2: Community-level functional interactions with fire track long-term structural development and fire adaptation
column7:
community level, fire, fire adaptation, fire severity,
flammability, functional expressions,  functional interactions and responses, paleoecology, plant community, structural development
column8:
Our focus is on morphological and
 structural traits that indicate sensitivity to changes  in fire activity.
When combined with reconstructed fire history, variation in the abundances
(i.e., scores) of  these functional attributes suggest selection of plant community
expressions that indicate community-level responses to fire related  to changes
in structural development and changes in fire adaptation.
column64:
Nine of the 14 functional attributes were significantly related
 to fire fre quency (number of fires 1,000 per year). When combined with fire history data,  variation in the abundances of functional attributes suggest selection of plant  community expressions that indicate community-level responses to fire related to  changes in structural development and changes in fire adaptation. Fire frequency  variations may drive directional selection for fire- adapted attributes, and against  fire-sensitive attributes. Our results indicate increased Holocene fire activity may  have been linked to vegetation functional interactions with fire.

column7:

column8:
The overarching question we work to
 address is, does the pollen-plant functional trait linkage represent
ecological function in North America?
column9: Functional trait data representing primary axes of plant strategy are used to assess spatial relationships between the pollen-plant functional trait linkage (i.e., estimates of site-level function via individual traits) and the functional trait data used to inform these pollen-derived values. We then use these functional estimates from North American sites to quantify functional diversity, the biodiversity metric that governs ecosystem function, and therefore the ability of ecosystems to provide services for humans (Diaz et al., 2007). To present a macroecological example of the utility of making the pollen-plant functional linkage, we compared pollen-based functional diversity estimates to those from modern tree assemblages in the form of latitudinal functional diversity gradient, a pattern found to be embedded within the latitudinal biodiversity gradient for the western hemisphere.

 sufficiently track ecological function in trait and geographic space and the macroecological biodiversity pattern was reconstructed, although there were minor differences between the slopes of the functional diversity and each of the pollen index gradients. Taken together, the outcomes of our investigation indicate reliability in extending the pollen-plant functional trait linkage into deeper time, at least for examining North American functional dynamics.

Temporal difference analysis is more meaningful when only sites 
containing samples for all time slices are analysed. During the mid
Holocene, southern Europe was more heavily forested with temperate 
vegetation than it is at present, but drought-tolerant xeric vegetation
 was still widespread along the southern margins of the region. 
Although human land use may have caused the degradation 
of land between the mid-Holocene and the present, the mere 
presence of xeric vegetation in the Mediterranean region 
does not require human impact. This challenges the commonly
 held belief that modern Mediterranean vegetation represents 
a ‘degraded’ state.

column2:
Functional Signatures of Surface Pollen and Vegetation Are
Broadly Similar: Good News for Past Reconstructionsof Vegetation
column7:
Arid Central Asia, biomes, communities, databases,
functional biogeography, past climate, plant traits,
 pollen, trait distribution
column8:
In this present study, we compare the trait values and distributions
of surface pollen assemblages and vegetation plots to test
(1) whether relationships between traits (univariate distributions of
trait values and pairwise trait–trait relationships) are conserved
between pollen and vegetation. We also test
(2) whether the functional space (i.e., the organisation of plant height,
seed mass, leaf area, specific leaf area, nitrogen content per
leaf mass, and stem-specific density trait values in
multivariate space) derived from surface pollen samples
matches that of extant vegetation (in vegetation plot records).
This is necessary since whole phenotypes rather than individual
traits respond to the environment (Anderegg 2023). Once
validated, it will be possible to (3) combine sediment pollen with
traits (Birks 2020) to assess the functional structure of past vegetation,
from which biomes could be reconstructed (Harrison et al. 2010).
column9:

types to reduce the biases between pollen
and vegetation-based functional spaces? 
2) Between plant species and pollen types,
do we observe  consistent trait distributions,
univariate relations between traits, and
multivariate phenotypic spaces?
3) Are the functional spaces derived from
pollen and extant  vegetation comparable
at the level of communities?

column2: Modelling Late Miocene vegetation in Europe: Results of the CARAIB model and comparison with palaeovegetation data
column7: Miocene, Europe, Vegetation, Model, Palaeovegetation data, Plant functional types, Biomes
column8: To develop a methodology to validate climate and dynamic vegetation model runs for Neogene time slices using available palaeoflora data at the plant functional type (PFT) level, rather than at the biome level.
column9:
1) Adapt the dynamic vegetation model CARAIB (CARbon Assimilation In the Biosphere) specifically to represent Miocene European vegetation by adding thermophilous tree types.  
2) Force the model with ECHAM5-MPIOM (COSMOS) Tortonian climate simulations to map potential tree distributions and metrics (biomass, NPP).  
3) Quantify and compare the simulated vegetation against a standardized macro/micro-palaeoflora presence/absence dataset using a custom PFT coexistence matrix.
column64: The CARAIB model produced an overall presence/absence agreement of 53% when evaluated across all sites and tree classes.  Boreal/Cold elements: The agreement was very high (>85%) for needle-leaved summergreen cold trees (Larix sp.). However, the model generally overpredicted the southern range of cold boreal/temperate trees, extending them further south than indicated by the fossil record.  Warm/Subtropical elements: The model correctly predicted the absence of tropical elements. However, it performed poorly (<40%) at capturing temperate perhumid warm trees. The model restricted subtropical trees to localized southern refugia, whereas the proxy data demonstrates these warm elements flourished across Central Europe up to 50°N.  Climate Model Implications: The systematic underprediction of warm elements suggests that the COSMOS-derived Late Miocene climate inputs suffer from a cold bias. This could stem from underestimated atmospheric CO2 levels or an overestimation of altered ocean heat transport caused by an open Panama seaway.

column2: A pollen-based pseudobiomisation approach to anthropogenic land-cover change
column7:

column8:
This paper proposes a method of intermediate complexity by which 
major land-cover changes can be assessed for individual sites and regions. 
A pseudobiomisation approach is used, in which pollen taxa are assigned 
to different land-cover classes (LCC), and the sum of adjusted pollen 
proportions for each class used to determine an LCC affinity score 
for individual pollen samples within stratigraphic pollen sequences. 
In a pilot study, data from pollen core sites from northwest Scotland 
and southwest England for the last 8000 cal. BP have been classified 
into ten land-cover classes, including predominantly wooded, 
semi-open and open types.
column9:
1) To assign pollen taxa to distinct land-cover classes (LCCs)
 that capture both natural and culturally modified open and wooded ground. 
2) To develop a mathematical affinity threshold framework
 that can successfully differentiate "semi-open" landscape 
categories from closed or completely open ground.  
3) To illustrate the methodology by applying it to 20 Holocene
 pollen sequences across two geographically contrasting 
regions of the UK (northwest Scotland and southwest England)
 over the last 8,000 years. .

column64:
Lake Mikata Vegetation Succession: Reconstructed three major evolutionary steps over the last 40,000 years:  
- Prior to 25,000 yr BP: Temperate deciduous forest (TEDE) continuously dominated the region. 
 - 22,000 to 18,000 yr BP: A total shift to cool-mixed forest (COMX) took place, representing the coldest stage of the Last Glaciation. This phase corresponds to modern-day southern Hokkaido climate and is flanked by brief transitional periods of rapid TEDE/COMX fluctuation.  
- Post 10,000 yr BP (Holocene): Warm-mixed forest (WAMX) was quickly established, mirroring modern-day environmental settings around the lake.

Modifying the biomization scheme to explicitly include a Disturbed
 Vegetation Biome (DISV) increased modern validation accuracy
 to 71% across Japan's human-altered landscape. In the Lake 
Mikata fossil core, two prominent phases of DISV were discovered.
 The first occurred during the late Holocene, fueled by human
 deforestation and intensive rice agriculture. The second occurred
 during the Last Glacial, where abrupt, severe natural climate
 fluctuations (resembling Dansgaard-Oeschger cycles) repeatedly
 damaged the forest canopy and enabled pioneer, disturbance-tolerant
 plant functional types to expand temporarily.

column7:

column8:
Here, fossil pollen data from Misty Lake
 on northeastern Vancouver Island, British Columbia (Lacourse 2005) 
that span the last 15 000 calendar years (cal yr) were used to investigate 
how the interaction between plant species traits and environmental 
conditions shaped the composition of postglacial forests.
column9: NA
column64:
Fourth-corner analyses revealed significant correlations 
between all paleoenvironmental variables (i.e., temperature, precipitation, summer insolation, vegetation density) and most plant traits (relative growth rate, minimum seed-bearing age, seed mass, height, life span, and shade, drought, and waterlogging tolerances). The strongest correlation was between paleotemperature and height, reflecting the positive effect of temperature on plant growth and development and the overarching competitive advantage that height confers. This research demonstrates that environmental conditions interact significantly with life-history and stress tolerance traits over long ecological timescales to determine forest composition. Climate is the ultimate control on postglacial forest composition and species abundances, but long-term community assembly is also constrained through interspecific differences in plant traits.

column64:
Species with similar life history traits tend to be most abundant at 
similar times and species that are functionally dissimilar are separated in time. 
For example, on central Vancouver Island, species with “fast” life history strategies 
(e.g., P. contorta, A. viridis) are most abundant in lateglacial plant communities, 
while those with “slow” life history strategies (e.g., T. heterophylla, T. mertensiana) 
are generally most abundant in mid-late Holocene forests. Within those time periods, 
sorting by elevation appears governed most strongly by differences in ecological 
performance and stress tolerance traits. This highlights that not only are species sorted 
through time as environmental changes occur but that species traits are also sorted on 
long timescales. Environmental change is the main driver of temporal turnover in forest 
composition, with this filtering acting on species traits to direct community assembly and 
dynamics.

Precipitation Maximum: Annual precipitation gradually increased during the early to mid-Holocene, showcasing a distinct Holocene Summer Monsoon Maximum (HSMM) relatively late, between ~5500 and 3100 cal yr BP.
Forcing Complexity: The delayed onset of peak monsoonal rainfall cannot be attributed to a single trigger, but indicates a complex internal climate mechanism driven by ice-sheet retreat, ENSO variations, and Okhotsk Sea-ice dynamics.

Modern Reference Baseline: The biomization algorithm accurately matched the modern potential natural forest or steppe biome across the majority of the 286 reference sites. It effectively distinguished between the cold conifer taiga of northern latitudes and the structurally diverse cool mixed forests characteristic of the southern Primorye zone.

Pre-LGM and Glacial Dynamics (ca. 32,000–18,000 yr BP): Reconstructed a landscape dominated by the Taiga biome mixed with prominent Tundra and Cold Steppe scores. This indicates a highly open, cold, and arid landscape during the Last Glacial Maximum, with cold-tolerant Larix and herbaceous communities dominating the Lower Amur valley.

Deglaciation and Holocene Recovery: * Early Holocene: Witnessed a clean transition to Cool Conifer Forest as temperatures warmed and moisture increased, facilitating the rapid expansion of Picea and Abies.

Mid-Holocene Climate Optimum: Marked by a significant expansion of the Cool Mixed Forest biome, indicating a substantial influx of thermophilous broad-leaved trees (e.g., Quercus, Ulmus, Tilia). This represents the warmest and most humid phase of the sequence, with temperatures higher than today.

Methodological Confirmation: The successful reconstruction demonstrates that the standard northern Eurasian biomization matrix can be reliably applied to the high-latitude Pacific edge of Asia, providing an objective framework for past climate model-data comparisons.

Last Glacial Maximum Conditions (22,000–18,000 cal yr BP): Reconstructed a severely restricted forest cover across China. Desert, Steppe, and Tundra biomes expanded heavily into northern and central China, while the limited forest biomes present were pushed south and east toward coastal refugia due to a cold, dry winter monsoon.

Deglaciation and Transitional Phase (14,000–11,000 cal yr BP): Marked by a progressive, steady expansion of temperate deciduous and conifer forests moving northward and inland as the climate warmed.

Holocene Climate Optimum (9,000–5,000 cal yr BP): Characterized by the maximum northward and westward extension of forest biomes. Broad-leaved evergreen and warm-mixed forests shifted north of their modern limits by roughly 1 to 3 degrees of latitude, indicating a highly active and expanded East Asian Summer Monsoon system bringing high moisture levels.

Late Holocene Decline (Last 2,000 years): Documented a sharp retreat of complex forest biomes and a massive expansion of open, human-dominated grasslands and steppe-like signals across eastern China, reflecting the combined effects of late Holocene cooling and widespread anthropogenic land clearance.

widespread expansion of open conifer woodlands across the Great Basin
 and modern southwestern deserts.

 linked to expansion lags of plants in northern Europe

column9:
Here, we explored the dynamics of observed
 and dark diversity, pool size and community completeness both at the taxonomic and functional diversity facets using a sedimentary pollen data from northern Europe. This data spans the last 14 500 years from the Late Glacial period until the present, a period during which the region has experienced significant changes in both climate and human land use
column64:
We found that taxonomic and functional observed and dark diversity 
increased over time, especially after the Late Glacial and during the Late Holocene. However, dark diversity dynamics revealed expansion lags related to species’ functional characteristics (dispersal limitation and stress intolerance) and an extensive functional redundancy when compared to taxa in observed diversity. We highlight that assessing observed and dark diversity dynamics is a promising tool to examine biodiversity change across spatial scales, its possible causes, and functional consequences.

They evaluate how climate and human disturbances affect
 community trait composition. We found weak relationships between environmental conditions and traits at the taxon level, but strong effects for community-mean traits. Overall, community-mean traits were more responsive to human disturbances than to climate change; humaninduced erosion increased the dominance of dense-wooded, non-zoochorous species with compound leaves, and human-induced fire increased the dominance of tall, zoochorous taxa with large seeds and simple leaves.

column64:
Community-weighted mean (CWM) wood density significantly increased,
 while maximum adult height and leaf area systematically decreased as 
elevation increased. These spatial trait shifts in the living vegetation 
were accurately predicted and captured by the relative abundance configurations of the 
modern surface pollen rain, demonstrating that pollen data can faithfully
 track major macroecological shifts in plant functional composition.
 Conversely, absolute species diversity trends (richness, Shannon, and Simpson) 
were poorly reflected in the uncorrected pollen data due to taxonomic 
constraints and variations in tropical pollen dispersal syndromes

column2:

column8:
Here, we use a functional trait approach
on timescales of 10,000 years to assess
 how climate and disturbances influence
 the community-mean adult height, leaf area,
 seed mass, and wood density for eight lowland
 and highland forest landscapes. To do so, we 
combine data of eight fossil pollen records 
with functional traits and proxies for climate
 (temperature, precipitation, and El Niño frequency) 
and disturbances (fire and general disturbances).
column9:
First, how do temporal changes in past 
climate (precipitation, temperature anomalies, 
and the frequency of El Niño events) and past 
disturbances (fire activity and disturbances in general) 
determine shifts in community-mean trait values across 
Neotropical forests over at least the last 2000 years 
(for some of our sites up to 12,000 years).
Second, how do these responses of trait composition 
to climate and disturbances depend on elevation?
column38:
WD database, BIEN,
Seed Mass Database
column64:
We found that temperature and disturbances were the most important 
drivers of changes in functional composition. Increased water availability
 (high precipitation and low El Niño frequency) generally led to more acquisitive
 trait composition (large leaves and soft wood). In lowland forests, warmer climates
 decreased community-mean height probably because of increased water stress, 
whereas in highland forests warmer climates increased height probably because of 
upslope migration of taller species. Disturbance increased the abundance of acquisitive, 
disturbance-adapted taxa with small seeds for quick colonization of disturbed sites, large 
leaves for light capture, and soft wood to attain fast height growth. Fire had weak effects 
on lowland forests but led to more stress-adapted taxa that are tall with fast life cycles and
 small seeds that can quickly colonize burned sites.

column64:
Reconstruction Accuracy: The updated biomisation scheme successfully assigned 124 out of 150 validation sites (82.6%) to their correct true biological biome, proving its high accuracy for high-biodiversity tropical regions.

Taxonomic Expansion Success: Expanding the lookup tables to include nearly all regional pollen types (excluding only aquatics, ferns, and exotics) significantly reduced old model errors that misclassified open savannas as closed forests.

Resolving Forest/Savanna Overlaps: The revised framework accurately distinguishes between closed dry forests, open moist savannas, and true Miombo woodlands (Zambezian dry forest), making it a highly reliable tool for future paleoclimate and vegetation reconstructions in East Africa.

column2: Holocene Neolithic human activity shaped ecosystem functions through the altering of vegetation traits in Zhejiang, eastern China
column7:
Pollen, Holocene, Vegetation traits, Landscape,
 Climate Human activities
column8:
The aim was to reconstruct past
 long–term changes in plant traits and to investigate variations in ecosystem functioning under the pressure of natural climate shifts and anthropogenic disturbances.
column64:
Our findings reveal that, during the Holocene, plant functional
 traits at natural sites gradually adapted to warmer and wetter conditions, with the natural functional dispersion (FDis) showing a steady decrease between 9.0 and 4.0 cal ka BP. At human–impacted sites, intensified human activities gradually transformed the landscape from forest to grassland and shrubland, allowing light–demanding vegetation to thrive due to extended canopy gaps. Pioneer plants flourished in the secondary succession, leading communities to exhibit more aggressive resource acquisition strategies compared to a natural community.

column64:
Inferred modern biomes matched historic maps well. At 6000 yr BP, 
biomes reflected warmer/drier interior conditions, but general 
biome positions stayed close to modern distributions, with no 
dramatic northward treeline shift. At 18,000 yr BP, steppe and 
tundra dominated the Midwest and northwest, while eastern 
forests were highly open, structurally altered, and dominated

column64:
The modern validation shows a high degree of fidelity with actual vegetation 
zones. At 6000 BP, a stronger summer monsoon forced forest biomes to 
expand extensively northward and westward; temperate deciduous forest
 shifted approximately 800 km north of its modern limit. At 18,000 BP, 
extreme cold and dry conditions eliminated temperate deciduous forests, 
moving steppe and desert biomes eastward to the modern coastline. 
Boreal taiga moved south into areas currently dominated by temperate 
ecosystems.

The biomization procedure successfully reproduced the modern 
east-to-west and latitudinal vegetation zones of China. At 6000 yr BP,
 forest zones shifted significantly northward (temperate deciduous 
forests moved 500–600 km and broad-leaved evergreen forests 
moved ~300 km north). Deserts and steppes shrank in the northwest, 
while forests climbed to higher elevations on the Tibetan Plateau, 
reducing the extent of alpine tundra.

column64:
Post-glacial warming caused a rapid expansion of forest canopy
 across Europe between 12,000 and 8,500 BP. A "green period" 
of maximum forest cover extent occurred between ~8,500 and 
6,000 cal. BP, where dense forest canopy dominated the continent. 
This peak was followed by a progressive, long-term deforestation 
trend that significantly accelerated after 1,500 BP due to escalating
 human land clearance. Deforestation patterns were strongly 
diachronic, with Western Europe and the British Isles becoming
 open landscapes thousands of years before the heavy forests
 of Eastern Europe were systematically cleared.

column2: Holocene vegetation evolution on the Tibetan Plateau: An integrated approach utilising plant functional types
column7: Tibetan Plateau, Holocene, Plant diversity, Climate change, Human activities
column8: To explore the large-scale vegetation patterns and regional plant diversity evolutionary characteristics across the Tibetan Plateau (TP) since the Holocene using a plant functional type (PFT) approach.
column9:
1) To compile and standardize high-resolution pollen records from 58 fossil sites across the plateau. 
2) To evaluate the relationship between past plant diversity, distinct climate shifts, and late-Holocene human activities. 
3) To identify the structural baseline of modern vegetation distribution patterns from past Holocene assemblages
column28: Late Quaternary Pollen Database of East Asia
column64: The results show that: (1) The overall plant diversity in the TP since the Holocene shows a trend of increasing in the early and middle stages and decreasing in the late stage. Plant diversity responded to the 8.2 and 4.2 ka B.P. cold events, while the continuous decrease in biodiversity since 1000 cal.a B.P. is related to human activities. Among them, the impact of human activities on plant diversity in the northern part of the plateau occurred earlier than that in the southern part. (2) During the Holocene, the pollen content at each fossil site on the plateau changed significantly, but the changes in pollen groups were not noticeable. Zonal PFTs persisted, and their proportions fluctuated slightly, indicating that the vegetation composition on the TP was relatively stable during this period. (3) From the perspective of regional vegetation distribution, the vegetation composition in the northern and southern parts of the plateau shows a relatively stable situation.
